name: Old World Buildings
description: The 'Old World Buildings' theory, prevalent in alternative history circles, posits that many grand, architecturally sophisticated buildings found worldwide were not built by the civilizations traditionally credited with them (e.g., Egyptians building the pyramids, Romans building Roman structures). Instead, proponents suggest a more advanced, globally interconnected 'Old World' civilization existed, predating known history, possessing superior technology, and responsible for these structures. This lost civilization supposedly suffered a catastrophic event, such as a mud flood or deliberate destruction, erasing most traces of its existence and leaving behind only these magnificent buildings. Evidence cited includes architectural anomalies (precise stonework, seemingly impossible feats of engineering), the prevalence of similar architectural styles across continents, the presence of 'orphan' structures that don't fit neatly into the historical narrative of the region, and accounts of unexplained or suppressed historical information. Mainstream scholarship attributes these buildings to the ingenuity and accumulated knowledge of the civilizations that are historically credited with their construction, emphasizing the gradual development of architectural techniques and the documented historical records of these societies. They attribute perceived 'anomalies' to misunderstandings of ancient construction methods, the effects of erosion and time, and the limitations of historical documentation. The theory often intertwines with narratives of suppressed history, secret societies, and deliberate misinformation campaigns designed to hide the 'true' history of humanity.
subtitle: Lost civilization's architectural legacy?

claims.claim: Grand buildings worldwide are evidence of a lost, advanced civilization.
claims.evidence: Architectural anomalies, similar styles across continents, 'orphan' structures.
claims.criticism: Attributed to known civilizations' ingenuity, gradual development, and documented history. 'Anomalies' explained by erosion, misunderstandings of methods, and limited documentation.

claims.claim: A global cataclysm, possibly a mud flood, destroyed this 'Old World' civilization.
claims.evidence: Evidence of sediment layers in urban environments, perceived inconsistencies in historical flood narratives.
claims.criticism: Sediment layers explained by natural geological processes (flooding, erosion), urban development, and construction activities. Historical flood narratives are often localized and exaggerated.

claims.claim: History has been deliberately suppressed to conceal the existence of this advanced civilization.
claims.evidence: Alleged gaps and inconsistencies in historical records, suppression of alternative theories.
claims.criticism: Historical records are incomplete and subject to interpretation, but the vast majority of evidence supports the conventional historical timeline. Alternative theories lack supporting evidence and often rely on speculation.

keyFigures.name: Anatoly Fomenko
keyFigures.role: Proponent of New Chronology, which supports radical revision of history.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Author promoting theories of lost civilizations and advanced ancient knowledge.

keyFigures.name: Michael Shermer
keyFigures.role: Science writer and skeptic, critical of pseudoscientific claims and historical revisionism.

mythologyConnections: Stories of lost continents and advanced civilizations

timeline.event: Emergence of comparative architecture and archaeology, leading to initial observations of similarities across cultures.
Rise of alternative history and conspiracy theories, including the first formulations of the 'Old World' concept.
Increased popularity of the theory online, fueled by social media and alternative media channels.

sources.author: Ronald H. Fritze
sources.work: Invented Knowledge: False History, Fake Science and Pseudo-religions
sources.title: Ronald H. Fritze
